learningGoal: How to spot 'young animal to adult animal' analogies — these come up loads, and some of the baby names are really surprising!
How to apply the pattern: baby is to adult as baby is to adult (once you see it, you can't miss it)

screens.title: puppy is to dog as kitten is to ___?
screens.body:
Daisy is working on word relationship puzzles.

Did you know that some baby animals have really unusual names? A baby swan is a cygnet, and a baby kangaroo is a joey! Word analogies work like this: **A is to B as C is to ___**.

The trick is to spot the **relationship** between the first pair, then apply the SAME pattern to the second pair. Let's have a go!

screens.title: Young to Adult
screens.body: **puppy** is to **dog** — can you see the connection? This is one of the most common analogy patterns: **baby animal → adult animal**. Once you spot it in the first pair, just apply the same idea to **kitten**.

interaction.sentence: An analogy is a puzzle where you find the ____ between two pairs of words
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Analogies are about finding the same relationship in both pairs. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — analogies are about the RELATIONSHIP between the word pairs!

steps.text: "puppy" is a young "dog"
steps.why: First pair: baby → grown-up

steps.text: So "kitten" must be a young ___?
steps.why: Apply the same relationship

steps.text: Answer: "kitten" is a young "cat" ✓
steps.why: A puppy is a young dog, just as a kitten is a young cat

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **chick** is to **hen** as **fawn** is to ___?

interaction.question: chick is to hen as fawn is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! A chick is a young hen. A fawn is a young deer. Both pairs follow the young-to-adult pattern. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "deer". A chick is a young hen. A fawn is a young deer. Both pairs follow the young-to-adult pattern. ✓

screens.title: Young → Adult pattern — you've got it!
screens.body: Nice work! You've cracked one of the most popular analogy patterns. Here's your quick-reference guide:

steps.text: 1. Ask: is the first word a BABY version of the second?
steps.why: puppy → dog, kitten → cat, cub → bear

steps.text: 2. If yes, the answer is the adult version of the third word
steps.why: Same relationship applied to the new pair

steps.text: 3. Learn the tricky ones: cygnet → swan, joey → kangaroo
steps.why: Some baby names are unusual — these come up often ✓

learningGoal: How to spot 'tool to person who uses it' analogies — think about who uses what at work!
How to apply the pattern: tool is to professional as tool is to professional (this one's really satisfying to crack)

screens.title: pen is to writer as brush is to ___?
screens.body:
Evie is working through analogy puzzles.

Think about the **first pair**: what is a "pen" to a "writer"? Imagine them at work together.

Once you can name the relationship, you can solve ANY analogy that uses the same pattern!

visualProps.label: What does the first pair have in common?

screens.title: Tool → User
screens.body: A **writer** uses a **pen**. This pattern links a **tool or instrument** to the **person who uses it**. So who uses a **brush**?

steps.text: A "writer" uses a "pen"
steps.why: First pair: tool → professional

steps.text: So who uses a "brush"?
steps.why: Apply the same relationship

steps.text: A "painter" uses a "brush" ✓
steps.why: A writer uses a pen to write, just as a painter uses a brush to paint

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **hammer** is to **carpenter** as **needle** is to ___?

interaction.question: hammer is to carpenter as needle is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! A carpenter uses a hammer. A tailor uses a needle. Both pairs link a tool to the professional who uses it. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "tailor". A carpenter uses a hammer. A tailor uses a needle. Both pairs link a tool to the professional who uses it. ✓

screens.title: Tool → User pattern — well done!
screens.body: Great work! You've added another pattern to your collection. When you spot a tool-to-user analogy:

steps.text: 1. Ask: is the first word a TOOL used by the second word?
steps.why: pen → writer, scalpel → surgeon, gavel → judge

steps.text: 2. If yes, the answer is the PERSON who uses the third word
steps.why: Who uses a brush? A painter!

steps.text: 3. Pick the most specific user, not just anyone related
steps.why: A brush → painter (not 'artist' or 'cleaner') ✓

learningGoal: How to spot 'part to whole' analogies — where one thing is a piece of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: part is to whole as part is to whole (think jigsaw pieces!)

screens.title: page is to book as key is to ___?
screens.body:
Zara is puzzling over a word relationship question.

Look at the first pair: **"page"** and **"book"**.

Is one a PIECE of the other? Like a slice from a cake or a brick from a wall? If so, you've found a **part-to-whole** analogy!

visualProps.label: Is the first word part of the second?

screens.title: Part → Whole
screens.body: A **page** is part of a **book**. In this pattern, the first word is a **small piece** of the second word. So what is a **key** part of?

steps.text: A "page" is part of a "book"
steps.why: First pair: a piece of the bigger thing

steps.text: So a "key" is part of a ___?
steps.why: What bigger thing contains this piece?

steps.text: A "key" is part of a "keyboard" ✓
steps.why: A page is one part of a book, just as a key is one part of a keyboard

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **wing** is to **aeroplane** as **sail** is to ___?

interaction.question: wing is to aeroplane as sail is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! A wing is part of an aeroplane. A sail is part of a boat. Both pairs link a piece to the whole thing it belongs to. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "boat". A wing is part of an aeroplane. A sail is part of a boat. Both pairs link a piece to the whole thing it belongs to. ✓

screens.title: Part → Whole pattern — nice one!
screens.body: Brilliant! You've got another analogy pattern in your toolkit now. When you spot a part-to-whole connection:

steps.text: 1. Ask: is the first word a PIECE of the second?
steps.why: page → book, wheel → car, brick → wall

steps.text: 2. If yes, the answer is the WHOLE THING that contains the third word
steps.why: A key is part of a keyboard!

steps.text: 3. Don't confuse part-whole with tool-user!
steps.why: A wheel is PART of a car, not a tool used by a car ✓

learningGoal: How to spot 'cause and effect' analogies — one thing leads to another, like dominoes!
How to apply the pattern: cause leads to effect as cause leads to effect

screens.title: heat is to melt as cold is to ___?
screens.body:
Evie is working through a verbal reasoning worksheet.

Look at **"heat"** and **"melt"**.

Does the first thing **cause** or **lead to** the second? Like how rain causes puddles, or how studying leads to good marks? If so, you've found a **cause-and-effect** analogy!

visualProps.label: Does the first word cause the second?

screens.title: Cause → Effect
screens.body: **heat** causes **melt**. In this pattern, the first word **causes** or **leads to** the second. So what does **cold** cause?

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Spot the cause in pair 1, then apply it to pair 2. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by identifying the cause-effect in the first pair.

steps.text: "heat" causes "melt"
steps.why: First pair: cause → result

steps.text: So what does "cold" cause?
steps.why: Apply the same cause-effect logic

steps.text: "cold" causes "freeze" ✓
steps.why: Heat causes things to melt, just as cold causes things to freeze

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **wind** is to **erosion** as **fire** is to ___?

interaction.question: wind is to erosion as fire is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Wind causes erosion. Fire causes ash. Both pairs link a cause to the result it produces. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "ash". Wind causes erosion. Fire causes ash. Both pairs link a cause to the result it produces. ✓

screens.title: Cause → Effect pattern — you've got it!
screens.body: Excellent! You've cracked another pattern. Cause-and-effect analogies are everywhere once you start looking for them:

steps.text: 1. Ask: does the first word CAUSE the second?
steps.why: heat → melt, rain → flood, practice → improvement

steps.text: 2. If yes, find what the third word CAUSES
steps.why: cold → freeze, study → knowledge

steps.text: 3. Pick the direct result, not just something related
steps.why: Cold causes freezing (not 'shiver' or 'winter') ✓

learningGoal: How to spot analogies where the relationship IS about words meaning the same (synonym) or the opposite (antonym) — two skills in one!
How to apply the pattern: word is to synonym/antonym as word is to synonym/antonym

screens.title: hot is to cold as big is to ___?
screens.body:
Daisy is working on word relationship puzzles.

Here's a neat twist: sometimes the relationship in an analogy is that the words are **opposites**! Everything you've already learned about synonyms and antonyms comes in handy here.

Look at **"hot"** and **"cold"** — do they mean the same thing, or opposite things?

screens.title: Synonym or Antonym?
screens.body: Are **"hot"** and **"cold"** synonyms (words that mean the same) or antonyms (words that mean the opposite)? Decide which, then apply the same rule to **"big"**.

steps.text: "hot" and "cold" are opposites
steps.why: They mean opposite things

steps.text: So find the antonym of "big"
steps.why: Apply the same relationship type

steps.text: "big" → "small" ✓
steps.why: Hot is the opposite of cold, just as big is the opposite of small

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **light** is to **dark** as **wet** is to ___?

interaction.question: light is to dark as wet is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Light and dark are opposites. Wet and dry are opposites. Both pairs follow the antonym pattern. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "dry". Light and dark are opposites. Wet and dry are opposites. Both pairs follow the antonym pattern. ✓

screens.title: Synonym & Antonym analogies — two birds, one stone!
screens.body: Well done! You've just connected two different skills together. When the relationship IS about word meaning:

steps.text: 1. Check: are the first pair synonyms or antonyms?
steps.why: happy/glad = synonyms, hot/cold = antonyms

steps.text: 2. Apply the SAME relationship to the second pair
steps.why: If pair 1 are opposites, pair 2 must be opposites too

steps.text: 3. Watch out for CLOSE but not exact matches
steps.why: angry → furious (synonym), NOT angry → sad (different emotion) ✓

learningGoal: How to spot 'animal to its home' analogies — did you know an otter's home is called a holt?
How to apply the pattern: animal is to its home as animal is to its home (there are some brilliant ones to learn!)

screens.title: bird is to nest as rabbit is to ___?
screens.body:
Evie is matching animals to where they live.

Did you know that lots of animals have special names for their homes? A **"bird"** lives in a **"nest"**. So what does a **"rabbit"** live in?

This is the **animal-to-home** pattern — a really popular one in the 11+!

visualProps.label: Where does the second animal live?

screens.title: Animal → Home
screens.body: A **bird** lives in a **nest**. This pattern links an **animal** to its **home or dwelling**. So where does a **rabbit** live?

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Spot the animal-to-home link, then apply it. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first spot how pair 1 connects, then apply the same pattern.

steps.text: A "bird" lives in a "nest"
steps.why: First pair: animal → its home

steps.text: So a "rabbit" lives in a ___?
steps.why: What is this animal's home called?

steps.text: A "rabbit" lives in a "burrow" ✓
steps.why: A bird lives in a nest, just as a rabbit lives in a burrow

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **squirrel** is to **drey** as **otter** is to ___?

interaction.question: squirrel is to drey as otter is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! A squirrel lives in a drey. An otter lives in a holt. Both pairs link an animal to its specific home. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "holt". A squirrel lives in a drey. An otter lives in a holt. Both pairs link an animal to its specific home. ✓

screens.title: Animal → Home pattern — brilliant!
screens.body: Well done! These animal homes are great to have in your back pocket for the exam. Learn the tricky ones and you'll be ahead of the pack:

steps.text: bird → nest, bee → hive, spider → web
steps.why: Common ones you probably know

steps.text: rabbit → burrow, fox → den, horse → stable
steps.why: Farm and countryside animals

steps.text: beaver → lodge, pig → sty, otter → holt
steps.why: Trickier ones that catch people out ✓

learningGoal: How to spot 'example to category' analogies — where one thing is a type of something bigger
How to apply the pattern: example is to category as example is to category (think sorting things into groups!)

screens.title: rose is to flower as Mars is to ___?
screens.body:
Daisy is working on grouping word puzzles.

Here's one you'll get the hang of quickly! Is **"rose"** a type of **"flower"**? If the first word is a **specific example** and the second is its **category**, you've spotted the pattern!

visualProps.label: What category does it belong to?

screens.title: Example → Category
screens.body: A **rose** is a type of **flower**. In this pattern, the first word is a **specific example** and the second is the **group or category** it belongs to. So what group does **Mars** belong to?

steps.text: "rose" is a type of "flower"
steps.why: First pair: example → category

steps.text: So "Mars" is a type of ___?
steps.why: What group does this belong to?

steps.text: "Mars" is a type of "planet" ✓
steps.why: A rose is a type of flower, just as Mars is a type of planet

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **violin** is to **instrument** as **eagle** is to ___?

interaction.question: violin is to instrument as eagle is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! A violin is a type of instrument. An eagle is a type of bird. Both pairs link a specific example to its category. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "bird". A violin is a type of instrument. An eagle is a type of bird. Both pairs link a specific example to its category. ✓

screens.title: Example → Category pattern — spot on!
screens.body: Another pattern in the bag! When you spot an example-to-category analogy:

steps.text: 1. Ask: is the first word a TYPE OF the second?
steps.why: rose → flower, guitar → instrument, oak → tree

steps.text: 2. If yes, find the CATEGORY that the third word belongs to
steps.why: Mars is a type of planet, not a type of star

steps.text: 3. Pick the most precise category, not too broad
steps.why: Salmon → fish (not 'animal' — too vague) ✓

learningGoal: How to spot 'creator to creation' analogies — who makes what? Think about people and the things they create!
How to apply the pattern: creator is to creation as creator is to creation

screens.title: author is to book as composer is to ___?
screens.body:
Evie is puzzling over who makes what.

Think about it: an **"author"** creates a **"book"** — that's their job! So what does a **"composer"** create?

This is the **creator-to-creation** pattern — matching people to the things they make. It's like a fun jobs quiz!

screens.title: Creator → Creation
screens.body: An **author** creates a **book**. In this pattern, the first word is a **person who makes things** and the second is **what they make**. So what does a **composer** create?

steps.text: An "author" creates a "book"
steps.why: First pair: maker → product

steps.text: So a "composer" creates a ___?
steps.why: What does this person make or produce?

steps.text: A "composer" creates a "symphony" ✓
steps.why: An author creates a book, just as a composer creates a symphony

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **author** is to **book** as **composer** is to ___?

interaction.question: author is to book as composer is to ___?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! An author creates books. A composer creates symphonies. Both pairs link the creator to what they produce. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "symphony". An author creates books. A composer creates symphonies. Both pairs link the creator to what they produce. ✓

screens.title: Creator → Creation pattern — well done!
screens.body: Fantastic! You've now got a whole collection of analogy patterns. When you spot a creator-to-creation connection:

steps.text: 1. Ask: does the first person MAKE the second thing?
steps.why: author → book, baker → bread, poet → poem

steps.text: 2. If yes, find what the third person MAKES
steps.why: composer → symphony, sculptor → statue

steps.text: 3. Pick the specific product, not the material or workplace
steps.why: Carpenter → furniture (not 'wood' or 'workshop') ✓
